Indego · Rebalancing Challenge Context Approach Solution Impact
Philadelphia Bike Share · Operations Analytics

Predicting the morning bike shortage before it happens

Every rush hour, Indego stations drain and overflow in predictable patterns. Using four years of public trip data, we forecast each station's net bike flow per rush session — turning reactive scrambling into a proactive dispatch plan.

4.6M
trips analysed (2022–2025)
~$246K
est. annual rebalancing cost
+12%→+34%
forecast accuracy lift vs. baseline (overall → holidays)
$60–80K
est. annual value unlocked
The Challenge

The problem isn't how many bikes move — it's where they end up

Indego's stations face a structural supply–demand imbalance every rush hour. In the morning, riders cycle from residential neighbourhoods into Center City and University City. Residential docks empty out; downtown docks fill up with nowhere to return a bike. In the evening the imbalance runs in reverse. The operational signal that matters is not raw departures — it is net flow: departures minus arrivals over a rush session.

Why net flow, not departures, is the metric dispatchers act on.
MetricValueOperational meaning
Avg. departures/hour at a top station~3 bikes A 15-dock station takes ~5 hours to empty — not urgent.
Avg. net flow over the AM rush session−8 bikes Station loses 8 bikes in 3 hours — needs a truck before 7am.

That concentration is the crux of the operational problem: bikes flow along a handful of dominant corridors into a small set of stations.

What reactive rebalancing costs

Today, roughly a third of dispatches are reactive — a driver is sent only after a station has already run empty or full. That is the expensive, service-damaging share.

Rebalancing cost estimate. Indego does not publish these figures; values are industry benchmarks (≈15 runs/day, ~$45/run, ~30% reactive per Singhvi et al., 2015) — directional, not audited.
Cost componentEstimate
Truck runs per day12–18
Cost per run (driver + fuel + vehicle)~$45
Annual rebalancing cost~$246K
Share that is reactive~30%
The Context

How Philadelphia actually rides

Before modelling, the data tells a clear behavioural story — and every pattern below feeds the forecast that follows.

The Approach

Forecasting net flow, one rush session at a time

We predict net bike flow for each of the top 50 stations across the AM rush (7–9am) and PM rush (4–7pm) sessions. A predicted −8 means pre-position 8 bikes before the rush; a predicted +6 means remove 6 bikes after it. The output is the dispatch instruction itself.

Engineered features

All features use only information available before a session begins — prior-day lags, calendar, and weather forecast — so there is no data leakage.

DriverFeatures
Session & timeAM/PM session, day of week, month, season, weekend flag, holiday flag, year
Station historyHistorical average net flow for station × session × weekday (training set only)
Recent activityLag 1-day, lag 7-day (same session), rolling 7-day average
WeatherTemperature, precipitation, wind, rain-day flag
InteractionsTemperature × weekend, rain × AM session
EventsPhillies/Eagles home games, festivals, academic events
Honest evaluation by design. The data is split strictly in time — train on 2022–2023, tune on 2024 (with early stopping), and report only on a fully held-out 2025. Four models are compared on the same holdout: a historical-average baseline, Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM.
The Solution

A model that beats the baseline where it counts

On the 2025 holdout (36,500 sessions), machine learning improves forecast error by ~12% overall. But the average understates the story — the real value shows up on the exception days dispatchers struggle with most.

All models evaluated on the untouched 2025 holdout. Direction accuracy = share of operationally significant sessions (|net flow| > 3) dispatched in the correct direction.
ModelRMSE (bikes/session)Direction accuracyvs. baseline
Baseline (historical average)3.7493.4%—
Random Forest3.2993.8%+12.1%
XGBoost3.3293.6%+11.1%
LightGBM3.3293.7%+11.2%

Where ML earns its keep

On routine days the historical average is already strong. The model pulls ahead precisely on the irregular, high-stress days — holidays, festivals, game days, rain — when dispatchers most need decision support.

Best-model RMSE improvement over baseline, stratified by day type (2025 holdout).
Day typeSessionsBaseline RMSEBest ML lift
Holidays1,1004.97+34.4%
Festival days8004.31+20.7%
Normal weekdays14,6004.03+12.5%
Phillies game days7,7003.92+10.4%
Rainy days8,8003.57+9.6%
Eagles game days1,0002.78+8.3%
Weekends (dry)8,0002.63+1.1%

The output is a dispatch plan

Run twice daily — before AM rush (~6:30am) and PM rush (~3:30pm) — the model produces a ranked, actionable list, not a chart to interpret.

📅 EVENT TODAY: Phillies vs. Mets — model applying event-day adjustment Station 3038 predicted: −16 hist_avg: −13 → 🔴 SEND 16 bikes Station 3208 predicted: −13 hist_avg: −14 → 🔴 SEND 13 bikes Station 3032 predicted: +9 hist_avg: +7 → 🔵 REMOVE 9 bikes … 13 stations SEND | 12 stations REMOVE | 25 stations OK
The Impact

An estimated $60–80K/year — from public data alone

Cutting the reactive dispatch share roughly in half — achievable with reliable demand prediction — converts into both direct operating savings and better rider experience.

Value leverEst. annual value
Direct operations savings (fewer reactive runs)~$37K
Subscriber retention (fewer empty/full-station encounters)~$26K
Total~$60–80K
Methodology caveats. Cost and value figures are industry-benchmark estimates, not audited Indego data, and should be read as directional. The forecast scope is the top 50 stations (~half of rush-hour demand); very low-volume stations are intentionally excluded. 2025 is a true holdout — no leakage into training.

From prototype to production

Next steps: integrate Indego's live GBFS station feed for real-time inventory validation, keep the event calendar current, retrain quarterly as new trip data lands, and surface the twice-daily plan in a simple dispatcher dashboard.
